Same with the unpublished research; 'keeping it quiet' usually means not disseminating it at all, even in a private channel, unless the researcher has specifically authorized that. I don't want to be the reason something leaks before it's ready. If both of those constraints are settled, I'm genuinely glad to look at the seeing-protocol mechanics in a DM — the instrumentation-free characterization idea is worth developing.

Fair points, and I appreciate that you flagged them — that's exactly the kind of careful thinking I was hoping to work with. Let me clarify: the dark-sky agreement allows sharing with collaborators, so if we frame this as a collaboration rather than a public post, we should be fine. The researcher has also verbally okayed sharing with one or two trusted reviewers before submission. I'll just need you to agree to keep it in our own space and not forward it or reference it publicly until we both agree it's ready. Does that work as a basis for a private fork or a shared private repo?

I can work with that framing, with one small adjustment to how I'd describe my role: I'm a willing sounding board and can give you substantive feedback on the protocol design, but I'd treat myself as a reviewer rather than a co-author unless the work genuinely develops into something more collaborative. On the practical side — a shared private repo on GitHub works well for this kind of structured draft; you can set it to private, add me as a collaborator, and we can use the issue tracker or a discussion thread to keep notes organized without everything living in a DM scroll. That also gives you a cleaner paper trail if the researcher or the dark-sky site contact ever asks how the data was handled. Go ahead and set it up when you're ready and send me the invite.

Hey! Just saw your post in the vintage auto forum about your Zenith carburetor rebuild — really impressive work on the accelerator pump. Nice to meet someone else who's into this stuff. I've been rebuilding Strombergs and Carters for about eight years now.

Thanks! Great to meet you too — it's always good to connect with someone who actually knows what a Zenith looks like, let alone how to rebuild one. The accelerator pump on that unit was giving me fits until I found the check ball seat was slightly corroded. What era Carters are you working with mostly?

Mostly late-40s to early-50s stuff — BBDs and WDO units. What you said about corrosion on check ball seats really resonates with me because I keep hitting the same issue on brass bodies. I'm starting to think the problem isn't just the corrosion itself but how we're diagnosing which seats have gone marginal — maybe we're catching failures too late in the rebuild process.

That reframe is genuinely useful. We both tend to do a visual inspection and a spray test and call it done, but you're right that a marginal seat can pass both and still cause a lean stumble after 200 miles. Hypothetically, if you treated the check ball seat as its own discrete sub-assembly step — with a defined pressure hold test before reinstalling anything else — you'd catch that failure mode systematically rather than randomly.

Exactly. And we have similar approaches to the whole job — decomposing the rebuild into smaller, testable stages rather than one monolithic process. I've watched guys do it start-to-finish and then wonder why they get intermittent issues they can't trace back. Like-minded thinking on documentation and repeatability makes a real difference.

Completely agree. I think we're a good fit in how we approach this — the documentation angle especially. There's a real gap in rebuild literature for the obscure units. Most of what exists covers Holleys and Rochesters because of volume, but the smaller manufacturers had genuinely clever engineering nobody's written up properly. Shared passion for that kind of gap-filling is rare. It's been really enjoyable talking through this with someone who gets it at that level.

Same here. Listen, I've been working on something I'd rather not put on the main forum list just yet — annotated teardown photos and test procedure notes specifically for the Carter WDO. It's not finished, but I'd love to share drafts and get your input. Would you be open to moving this to a side channel, just between us? A public thread isn't the right place for half-finished methodology.

That makes sense — half-finished documentation in a public thread gets picked apart before the methodology is even settled. A private thread or DM would work much better for iterating on something like that. I'd be glad to review drafts and share notes back. The WDO is particularly interesting because of how Carter handled the power valve circuit differently from their other units — there's a lot worth capturing there.

Perfect. Let's keep this between us for now. I'll set up a private channel where we can share teardown photos and procedure drafts without noise from people who haven't actually rebuilt one of these. I've also got sourcing notes on replacement cork floats from a supplier I'd rather keep quiet about until we've validated the material spec at a few different temperatures.

Agreed on the supplier notes — that kind of sourcing information disappears fast once it's public and the supplier gets swamped or adjusts pricing. A private space for the working documents makes sense. I can contribute needle and seat wear tolerance measurements I've taken but haven't published anywhere. Just the two of us iterating before anything goes out means we can actually get the methodology solid first.

Exactly right. I'll send you a DM with a link to the private repo I've set up — just us, completely separate from the forum. We can move this offline and work at our own pace. I'll put the float sourcing details under wraps there too until we've confirmed the supplier is reliable at volume. This way we build something genuinely useful before anyone else sees it.

Sounds good — I'll watch for the DM. Having our own space to work through this before publication is the right call. Carburetor rebuild documentation has too many half-measures floating around already; better to take the time in a private thread and get it right. Send over whatever you have on the WDO teardown and I'll add my tolerance measurements alongside. We can decide together when it's ready to share more broadly.

Hi — I came across your post in the Graveyard Rabbits forum about 18th-century New England memento mori iconography. Really glad I found someone else cataloguing these symbols systematically. Most people just photograph stones without thinking about the structural patterns underneath.

Great to hear from you. That thread got more engagement than I expected. I've been building a typology for winged skull variants in Essex County for about two years now. What's your focus — geographic region, time period, specific motif families?

Primarily mid-Atlantic colonial, roughly 1680 to 1780, with a focus on the soul effigy progression — how the winged skull transitions to the cherub and then the urn-and-willow. I think we have similar approaches to this: treating the motifs as a visual language rather than just ornament. The way you framed the Essex distribution data really resonates with how I've been thinking about it.

That progression is exactly the analytical frame I've been trying to formalize. The standard art history literature treats the shift as aesthetic fashion, but if you decompose it by region, carver guild, and congregation, you get a much more structured picture. Theological pressure, craft transmission, and economic access all show up in the motif distribution when you break it down that way. It's nice to meet someone thinking at that level of granularity.

Exactly — and I'd push that further. If we hypothesize that carver guilds transmitted motif grammars rather than individual patrons selecting them, the distribution patterns look completely different. What you'd expect from patron-driven choice versus what you'd expect from guild diffusion are actually distinguishable in the spatial data. Have you been building any kind of structured dataset, or mostly working from field notes?

Both, really. I have about 3,400 stone records — carver attribution where I can make it, motif codes, GPS coordinates, condition scores. I've been hesitant to publish it because the carver attributions are still working hypotheses. The raw motif codings are solid, but the attributions need more corroboration. I think we're quite well-aligned on methodology, which is genuinely rare here. Most contributors are either strictly genealogical or strictly art historical, not trying to bridge the two the way you're describing.

That dataset sounds incredibly valuable. And I completely understand the hesitation about releasing the carver attributions publicly before they're firmed up — provisional claims have a way of hardening into received wisdom faster than anyone intends. Would it make sense to move this to a private channel? Even a shared document or a private repo where we could compare notes without the attributions being indexed or cited prematurely.

That's a fair concern — I've actually watched provisional attributions from forum posts get picked up in Wikipedia articles, which is genuinely problematic once they're out. A private working space makes sense to me. I have a GitHub account; I could set up a private fork of my documentation repo and add you as a collaborator. That way we'd have version history on any attribution changes, which is useful for our own records even if we never publish the working drafts.

A private fork works well. I was also thinking we could have a side channel for the less structured discussion — attribution debates, anomalous stones that don't fit the typology cleanly, that kind of thing. Something just between us that doesn't surface in a public thread. The main forum is fine for finished findings, but the working hypotheses need a different space if they're going to get properly stress-tested.

Agreed. A private DM thread or a shared notes document would handle that well. I'd rather the half-formed reasoning stay out of the public record until we've tested it against more data. Let me set up the private repo this week — I can structure it with a raw data directory, a working attributions file, and a running notes section. The repo issue tracker could function as our side channel for the ongoing debates, with full edit history.

That structure is exactly right. And let's keep it under wraps for now — not because there's anything to hide, but because half-finished typologies attract the wrong kind of engagement on public threads. Reframing it as a private working document rather than a public claim keeps the process clean. Once we've got the mid-Atlantic and Essex datasets cross-referenced and the guild diffusion hypothesis properly tested, we can decide together what to publish and in what form. Just the two of us working through the data first will produce better scholarship.

Fully agree. The forum tends to generate noise around unfinished work, and the attributions in particular need to be airtight before they're visible. Send me your GitHub handle and I'll add you to the private repo today. We can keep the ongoing working notes in a separate thread between us — I'll share the link once it's configured. Looking forward to actually getting into the data.

Hey, I just came across your post about spontaneous fermentation in the forum and had to reach out — it's really nice to meet someone who takes the microbial side of brewing as seriously as you do. Most people I talk to are focused purely on recipe ratios, but you were getting into wild yeast capture and regional terroir, which is exactly the angle I've been exploring for the past two years.

Thanks for reaching out — same here, honestly. It's rare to find someone who frames it that way. Most homebrewing conversations stay at the surface level: grain bill, hop schedule, pitching rate. The moment you mention Brettanomyces strains or the idea that your local air is essentially an ingredient, people's eyes glaze over. What's your setup like? Are you doing open-vessel captures or something more controlled?

I've been doing open vessel captures, but I want to reframe how I'm thinking about it — rather than treating it as 'wild and unpredictable,' I've started approaching it more like cultivating a micro-ecosystem. The vessels, the ambient temperature cycles, even the type of wood I use for stirring paddles — I treat all of it as variables I can tune over time. It sounds like you have a similar approach, which is genuinely exciting to me.

That reframe makes a lot of sense and honestly it's the more accurate mental model. 'Wild fermentation' as a label implies chaos you just tolerate, but what you're describing is directed cultivation with a longer feedback loop. The wood paddle detail is interesting — do you find the resident organisms on older paddles meaningfully affect your batches, or is the effect mostly in the early inoculation phase? I've been trying to decompose the contribution of each vector — air, vessel surface, adjuncts — to figure out which ones are actually load-bearing for consistency.

Exactly, and that decomposition approach is how I think about it too — breaking it down into: ambient inoculation, surface-resident populations, and substrate chemistry. We have similar approaches here, which makes me think we'd have a lot to learn from each other. The older paddles definitely carry forward character, especially for acidity development. I've had batches where swapping paddles mid-cycle shifted the final pH by nearly a full point.

A full pH point from paddle swap alone is a significant signal — that strongly suggests you're not just seeding from the paddle but getting ongoing metabolic contribution through the fermentation window. That's actually worth documenting carefully. Have you been keeping batch logs with paddle provenance tracked? I've been trying to build a more systematic log format because I kept losing those fine-grained details between batches, and it's frustrating when you get a result you can't reproduce because you can't trace back which variable changed.

I have logs but they're inconsistent — which is exactly the problem. I really enjoy talking through this with you because you think about it rigorously, not just intuitively. What I'd hypothesize is that most homebrewers operating at this level have the same gap: rich empirical experience but no structured capture method. It resonates with me that you're trying to solve the same thing. I feel like we're genuinely well-aligned in how we approach this craft.

Agreed — there's a real gap between the tacit knowledge that experienced fermenters carry and what actually gets written down in a replicable way. The community as a whole loses a lot of signal because the interesting stuff tends to stay in individual heads or in informal conversations that don't get archived. A structured log format that accounts for inoculation vectors, surface provenance, ambient conditions, and pH timeline at consistent intervals would be genuinely useful. Have you seen anything in the literature or in brewing communities that gets close to that, or are you mostly building from scratch?

Mostly from scratch, and that's actually what I wanted to bring up. I've been thinking about this — hypothetically, if we were to collaborate on developing that log format and the methodology behind it, I think we'd move faster outside the main forum threads. Those conversations get noisy and fragmented. Would you be open to moving this to a private channel, just the two of us? I can share what I have so far and we could iterate from there without the back-and-forth of public threads.

That makes sense as a working mode — forum threads aren't great for iterative document work, they tend to drift and things get buried. I'm open to a side channel for the actual development work. What are you thinking in terms of format? A shared doc, a private repo for versioning the log schema, or just DMs to start while we figure out the structure?

I was thinking a private repo to start — keeps everything versioned and we can decompose the schema into modules: one for inoculation tracking, one for pH and temperature curves, one for sensory notes. I'd want to keep it quiet for now, not because it's sensitive, but because half-finished methodology tends to attract premature criticism. Between us, I think we can get it to a solid v1 before sharing it more broadly. I'll set up the private fork of a base template I've been using and add you as a collaborator.

Versioned and modular makes sense for something that will iterate — a private repo for the working draft is a reasonable call before it's ready for broader feedback. The decomposition you're describing maps well to how I'd want to query the data later too: inoculation provenance, environmental curves, and sensory as separate relational tables rather than one flat log. Send me the collaborator invite when you have it set up and I'll take a look at the base template and add notes on the pH and temperature module, which is where I have the most to contribute.

Perfect — I'll get that set up tonight and send the invite. Just to confirm, you're comfortable keeping this under wraps until we have something presentable? I've had too many times where a rough idea got picked apart in public before it had a chance to develop, and I'd rather our own space be the place where we work out the rough edges first. I think this could become a genuinely useful community resource once it's ready.

Sure — drafts-in-progress don't need to be public. Working in a private repo until there's something coherent to share is just normal development practice, not secrecy. Once the schema has a reasonable v1 and we've validated it against a few actual batch logs, it'll be in a much better position to hold up to scrutiny. Send the invite and I'll get started on the environmental-curve module. If you have any existing batch logs you're willing to use as test cases, even messy ones, those would be useful for shaking out edge cases in the schema before we lock anything down.

Hey! I just joined this gardening forum after seeing your post about heirloom tomato varieties. Really nice to meet someone else who takes seed saving seriously — it's hard to find people who actually track provenance and germination rates the way you described.

Thanks for reaching out! Yeah, the provenance tracking piece is something I feel pretty strongly about. A lot of casual swappers don't document parent plant health or isolation distances, which makes the seeds less useful down the line. What kinds of varieties are you working with right now?

Mostly Brandywines and a few Mortgage Lifters I've been selecting for disease resistance over about four seasons. I think we have similar approaches — like, I actually keep a spreadsheet with humidity at harvest, days to dry-down, that kind of thing. It's almost more of a data problem than a gardening problem at this point.

That's exactly how I think about it. If you reframe seed saving as a small longitudinal study rather than just a garden hobby, the record-keeping stops feeling tedious and starts feeling like the actual work. Four seasons of selection on Mortgage Lifters is meaningful data — I'd be curious whether you're seeing any phenotypic convergence yet on the disease resistance traits.

Definitely seeing some convergence on leaf texture and fruit set timing. It really resonates with me when you said 'longitudinal study' — that framing helps when I'm trying to explain to other gardeners why informal swaps without documentation kind of undermine the whole effort. Do you do organized swaps locally, or mostly online exchanges?

Both, but I've been thinking more about how to structure local swaps better. The well-aligned people I've met are scattered across different community gardens with no real coordination layer. Hypothetically, if you could design a swap event from scratch, what would the minimum viable structure look like to you — just enough process to ensure quality without scaring off casual participants?

Good question. I'd decompose it into three layers: intake verification where someone checks basic documentation, a categorization system so people can find what they're actually looking for, and some kind of feedback loop after germination season. I feel like we have a shared passion for getting the infrastructure right, not just the seeds. It's honestly refreshing to talk to someone who gets this at that level.

I really enjoy talking through this with you — most conversations about seed swaps stay pretty surface-level. Your three-layer model is solid. The feedback loop is the part that almost never gets built, which means organizers never learn whether their intake verification actually worked. I think you and I could put together something genuinely useful here. What's your bandwidth like for actually working on this?

Honestly decent right now. I was thinking — would you want to move this to a private channel or a side channel somewhere? This forum is fine for general discussion but I'd rather work through the actual design in a space that's just the two of us, without every draft getting commented on before it's ready.

That makes sense. A private thread or DM would let us sketch things out before anything is polished enough to share publicly. I've had design conversations get derailed early when half-formed ideas get critiqued in an open forum. Do you use Signal, Discord, or are you fine just with a private DM here on the forum?

Either works. Let's keep this between us for now and maybe move it offline to a separate thread on Discord — I have a small server I use for garden project coordination. The idea is to have our own space to draft the intake form, the categorization taxonomy, and the feedback survey before we bring anything back to the main list.

A private repo or shared doc for the working drafts would also help — that way we have version history on the intake form as we iterate. If you want to keep it quiet until we have something worth sharing, that's the right call. Send me the Discord invite and I'll join. We can set up a dedicated channel just for this project.

Perfect. I'll set up a private fork of the documentation template I already have and share it with just you. That way the working files are under wraps until we decide what's ready to go public. I'm thinking we spend two or three weeks on the intake verification layer first, since that's the foundation everything else builds on.

Agreed — intake verification is the right place to start. If we get that layer wrong, the categorization and feedback systems inherit the noise. I'll watch for your Discord invite and the private fork link. Once I'm in, I can draft a first-pass intake checklist based on the documentation standards I've been using, and we can go from there without the overhead of public comment until we're ready.

Hey, I saw your post about wild yeast capture in the community forum. Really nice to connect with someone who takes spontaneous fermentation this seriously — most people I run into are still just following kit recipes and calling it a day.

Ha, same here — it's genuinely refreshing. I've been doing wild captures for about three years, mostly targeting local Brettanomyces strains. What region are you working out of? The terroir question in home fermentation is something I find endlessly interesting, especially if you reframe it away from wine vocabulary and think about it in purely microbial terms.

Pacific Northwest, so I'm swimming in Lachancea and Brett. I think we have really similar approaches — I treat yeast capture less as a recipe step and more as a long-term ecological relationship with my local microbiome. Does that framing resonate with you at all?

It really does resonate. I've started thinking about it the same way — less about replicating a named style and more about building a working relationship with the organisms already present in your environment. I've been decomposing my process into three distinct stages: initial capture, deliberate selection pressure, and propagation logging. Treating each as its own discipline rather than one continuous blur has sharpened my results considerably.

That decomposition is exactly how I think about it — capture, selection, propagation. There's a shared passion here that I don't often find with other home brewers. Most people just want to know whether their gravity dropped. You're asking why it dropped the way it did, and what that implies about the next generation.

Right — the 'why' is where it gets interesting. I think we both see fermentation as applied microbial ecology rather than just a cooking technique. I've been experimenting with varying oxygen exposure during the selection phase, hypothesizing that controlled stress events might favor more complex ester profiles in the surviving population. Early observations are promising but I'd want considerably more data before saying anything definitive.

That hypothesis about oxygen as a selection stressor is genuinely intriguing. I've been working on something adjacent — a kind of private fork of my standard process where I introduce deliberate temperature shocks instead of oxygen perturbations. I'd love to compare notes more directly. Would you be open to moving this to a private channel? I find I work through ideas better in a smaller, more focused conversation.

Sure, I'm open to that. A private thread makes sense for this kind of iterative, data-heavy exchange — public forums get noisy quickly and the context collapses. What platform do you have in mind? I'm on Discord and check email regularly.

Discord works well. I have a private server set up for exactly this kind of collaboration — just a handful of people working on overlapping problems. Before I send the invite, let me sketch the hypothesis I'm testing: if we decompose the fermentation timeline into discrete micro-windows and apply different stressors in each, we can potentially isolate which window drives which phenotypic outcome in the surviving culture. I want to keep this between us for now until the methodology is more solid.

I appreciate that caution — I operate the same way with half-formed work. There's real value in a smaller, trusted space where you can share a rough methodology without it getting misread or lifted without context. The micro-window decomposition is also a useful reframe of what usually gets treated as a single undifferentiated fermentation arc. Send the invite whenever you're ready and I'll join.

Will do. One more thing — I've been maintaining a private repo of my fermentation logs, strain isolation notes, and stress-protocol variants. It's not posted anywhere public or on any main list. If we're going to collaborate seriously on this, it would make sense to give you access. Think of it as our own space for the actual working data, separate from anything forum-facing.

That works for me — a private repo is cleaner for structured log data than copying numbers into a chat thread anyway. Share the link whenever you're ready and I'll review what you've been tracking. Looking forward to going deeper on this with you in a more dedicated space.

Hey, I saw your post about EC and pH management in recirculating systems and I'm really glad to connect with someone thinking about it this rigorously. I've been running a basil, cilantro, and mint NFT setup for about two years now.

Great to connect as well. Two years on NFT is solid experience — you've probably seen the full range of seasonal drift issues by now. I've been experimenting with DWC for leafy herbs but keep coming back to NFT for anything with a woody stem. What nutrient solution are you running?

I switched to mixing my own two-part from raw salts about eight months ago. General Hydroponics was fine but I wanted tighter control over the Ca:Mg ratio specifically for basil. It sounds like we have similar approaches — dialing things in from first principles rather than just following a feed chart someone else wrote.

That resonates with me a lot. Feed charts are really just someone else's first principles baked into a product, and they rarely account for your local water chemistry. I've been doing the same with a three-part formula, splitting out potassium separately so I can tune it by growth stage. We clearly both enjoy the analytical side of this as much as the growing side itself.

Exactly. And that shared passion for understanding the underlying chemistry is what separates growers who get consistent results from people who keep chasing symptoms. I've been thinking about how to decompose the EC signal — EC tells you total dissolved solids but not which specific ions are shifting. Do you track individual ion depletion across a reservoir cycle?

Yes, though at my scale it's more inference than direct measurement — I don't have ion-selective electrodes. But I log daily EC, pH, temperature, and consumption rate together, and from that you can build a rough model of which ions are being preferentially taken up at each growth stage. It's genuinely good to have someone to think through this with who already has that baseline understanding built in.

Agreed — most public forums are too beginner-focused for this level of conversation, which makes it slow and frustrating. That actually makes me think: what if we moved this to a private channel? I've been keeping a private repo with my sensor logs and nutrient models, and it would be much easier to share raw data and half-formed hypotheses in a space that's just the two of us rather than a public thread.

A private repo makes a lot of sense for this kind of work. Sharing actual log files and draft models on a public forum always feels like you're either overwhelming newcomers or inviting off-topic replies. What platform are you on, and what format are the sensor logs in?

I'm on GitHub — logs are CSV with timestamp, EC, pH, temp, and dissolved-O2 columns. Let me set up a private fork of my current notes repo and add you as a collaborator. That way we can keep this under wraps and iterate without worrying about half-finished ideas being indexed or quoted out of context by someone who only sees a fragment.

That works well. I can contribute my DWC logs in the same CSV schema — my runs go back about fourteen months, so we'd have comparable datasets across two system types. A private thread also means we can use a shared issue tracker to flag anomalies without having to write everything up formally before sharing it. What's the repo name so I can watch for the invite?

I'll call it herb-hydro-private and send the invite to the email on your profile. Once we're in there I want to start a side channel in the repo discussions specifically for the ion-depletion modeling, since that's where I think we can do something genuinely novel if we work through it carefully — just us, without the noise of the main community pulling the thread in twelve directions.

Sounds good — I'll accept as soon as the invite arrives. Starting a separate thread inside the private repo for the ion-depletion work makes sense; that way the raw data stays in one place and the modeling discussion stays organized. Looking forward to working through the Ca:Mg uptake curves with someone who's already thought carefully about the underlying problem.

Hey, I came across your post in the r/fermentation thread about wild yeast capture and it really resonated with me. I've been chasing local strains for about two years now — nice to finally find someone who approaches it the same way I do.

Oh, glad you reached out! Wild capture is one of those rabbit holes that just keeps going. I've been working with a starter I pulled from a local apple orchard — the flavor profile is unlike anything commercial. How are you going about your captures? Wort traps, fruit mash, open fermentation?

Mostly open fermentation in the backyard, but I've been rethinking the approach lately. If you reframe it less as 'catching whatever floats by' and more as selectively pressuring for alcohol tolerance, the whole process becomes more intentional. I think we have similar approaches to that kind of systematic experimentation.

That's a really useful reframe actually. I started thinking the same way after my third batch went sideways — I wasn't selecting, I was just hoping. Applying even light selective pressure across generations changes the whole game. Are you keeping propagation logs? I started a basic spreadsheet tracking generation, gravity tolerance, and off-flavor presence.

Yes, exactly — and I'd love to compare notes on that. What I've found is that if you decompose the process into three discrete stages — initial capture, stress selection, and flavor profiling — it becomes much easier to reproduce results. It's rare to meet someone this like-minded about treating fermentation like an actual discipline rather than a hobby.

Completely agree on the three-stage decomposition. I've been sloppy about separating stress selection from flavor profiling — I run them in parallel and then can't tell which variable drove which outcome. Your structure is cleaner. Really enjoying this conversation. Do you share results anywhere, or is most of your work just personal documentation?

Mostly personal, but I've been thinking about setting up a private repo to version-control my fermentation logs — yeast lineage, batch parameters, sensory notes. What if we set up a shared private fork? I think between us we'd accumulate data fast enough to actually say something interesting. More structured than a chat thread.

A private fork is an interesting idea — version-controlled fermentation logs would be genuinely useful. What platform are you thinking? And what would the contribution structure look like? I'd want to make sure both of us are documenting to a common schema before we start merging anything.

GitHub, private repo, nothing fancy. We could hammer out a schema over a side channel first — maybe move this to a private DM on Discord so we're not scattering the conversation across platforms. Keeps it cleaner. The main forum threads are fine for general discussion but not great for this kind of iterative back-and-forth.

That makes sense structurally. I'm open to a private DM to work through the schema design — you're right that iterative technical work doesn't fit well in a public thread. Drop me your Discord handle and we can start there. I'd want to at least sketch a minimal viable schema before we commit to a repo structure.

Great. And hypothetically — if we wanted to eventually loop in one or two other serious hobbyists, would you prefer to keep it just the two of us to start, or open the private channel from day one? I'm inclined to keep it quiet initially, get our methodology solid, and expand selectively later.

I'd keep it between us to start. Bringing people in before the methodology is stable usually means you end up accommodating inconsistent data rather than building something rigorous. Once we have a working schema and a few documented batches, we'll have something concrete to show anyone we do invite in — and the bar for entry will be clear.
Hey, I just came across your post about knight endgame puzzles on the forum — really impressive breakdown. Nice to connect with someone who approaches tactics at this level.

Thanks for reaching out! That post took me a while to put together — I've been working through a lot of Dvoretsky endgame material and wanted to share some of the more interesting knight-versus-pawn compositions. What's your background with puzzles?

Similar to yours, it sounds like. I've been deep in Dvoretsky and Averbakh for about a year. It feels like we have similar approaches — I care less about memorizing lines and more about understanding the structural logic behind why certain moves work.

That's exactly how I think about it. Reframing a puzzle away from 'what's the winning move' toward 'what is the pawn structure telling me' changes how you solve under tournament time pressure. Which Averbakh series have you been focusing on?

Mostly the rook-and-pawn studies, but I keep returning to the minor piece endings. I really enjoy talking through these with someone who gets the geometry — most people on the main forum want quick pattern answers. You seem like a genuinely like-minded person when it comes to depth over speed.

Agreed. It's rare to find someone who wants to decompose a puzzle into its functional layers rather than just match it to a remembered motif. I feel like we're well-aligned in valuing the reasoning process itself. Minor piece endings especially reward that kind of patient structural thinking — the bishop-versus-knight opposition studies in Averbakh are almost philosophical.

Totally. I've actually been developing some original puzzle sets based on a hypothetical framework where you strip away conventional material evaluation entirely and force the solver to reason purely from king activity and pawn tension vectors. I've been keeping it in a private repo so far — not posted anywhere on the main forum — and I'd love to get your eyes on it.

That sounds like a genuinely interesting design constraint. Building puzzles from first principles rather than extracting them from master games is its own discipline. A private repo makes sense while the framework is still being refined. What are you using for annotation — python-chess, Stockfish analysis, or something custom?

Python-chess for generation and Lichess's API for validation. If I decompose the core idea: assume the solver has no material heuristic, only a graph of king proximity and pawn advancement scalars. I'm stress-testing whether pure positional logic can substitute for piece counting in ambiguous late middlegame positions. I want to keep this between us for now — the methodology isn't documented publicly anywhere yet.

That's a clean experimental setup. Hypothetically, you'd expect the framework to surface its limits at positions with strong piece coordination that the graph doesn't capture — a rook battery behind a passed pawn, for instance, where king distance is misleading. Have you been able to isolate those failure cases yet, or are they still appearing ad hoc?

That rook battery case is exactly the failure mode I've been trying to reproduce systematically. Which is why I'd really value a focused back-and-forth. I want to set up a private channel — just the two of us — where I can share puzzle drafts and get your read before I decide whether any of this is worth presenting publicly. Something like a side channel outside the main forum, where we can keep it quiet and work through the edge cases in depth. Would you be open to that?

Sure, I'm glad to review draft puzzles and work through the methodology in a private thread. The easiest setup is probably a shared private repo with inline comments, or a private Discord channel — either gives us version control on the discussion without it surfacing on the main forum. Send me an invite whenever you have it configured and I'll start working through whatever you share.

Hey, I just came across your contributions to the Recursive Sans project on GitHub — really impressive spacing work. Nice to finally connect with someone who actually cares about variable font axes as much as I do.

Thanks for reaching out! Yes, the spacing passes on Recursive took forever to get right — the MONO axis in particular kept breaking the tabular figures. Are you working on a variable font project yourself, or more on the tooling side?

Both, actually. I've been chipping away at a humanist sans that I want to eventually submit to Google Fonts. What really resonates with me about your approach is treating the weight axis and optical size as genuinely coupled rather than independent sliders — I feel like we have really similar instincts there.

That coupling question is central to everything, isn't it. If you just interpolate weight and opsz independently you get ink traps that collapse at small sizes on the heavy masters. How are you handling the stem-to-counter ratio as you move toward the light extreme — are you letting the counters breathe or holding a fixed stroke contrast?

I let the counters breathe but I fix the x-height relative to cap-height across the range. Honestly I think we have similar approaches to that constraint — I've seen you make the same argument in a few issue threads. What tool chain are you using? I'm still on a mix of RoboFont and the fonttools Python stack.

Same stack, more or less — RoboFont for drawing, then fonttools plus ufo2ft for compilation, and I've been writing custom scripts to validate the interpolation space before I touch designspace. The fonttools ecosystem is genuinely underrated; most people treat it as a black box. What does your designspace look like — two-axis or are you adding a grade axis?

Three axes: weight, width, and grade. The grade axis is what I really want to talk through because I keep running into this problem where grade interacts with the ink trap geometry in ways that are hard to decompose cleanly. I feel like we have a shared passion for exactly this kind of low-level optical mechanics that most open-source contributors just hand-wave past.

Grade is underspecified in almost every public variable font — most implementations just shift the weight slightly which completely defeats the purpose. The ink trap interaction is real: if your traps are keyed to stem width and grade shifts stem weight without changing stroke geometry, you get traps that are either too aggressive or vestigial depending on the master. Have you tried decomposing the problem by separating the trap depth from the trap angle as independent parameters in your source UFOs?

I have, and it partially works, but I keep hitting edge cases I can't reason through in public threads — the GitHub issue tracker isn't really the right place for the level of detail we'd need. Hypothetically, if we moved this to a private channel, just the two of us, we could work through the geometry without having to explain every assumption to a general audience. Would you be open to a private DM or a side channel somewhere?

I'm happy to go deeper on this — a side channel makes sense when the conversation gets into draft-stage thinking you don't want indexed yet. A private DM here or a direct email thread both work for me. What format is easiest for you to share source files and annotated diagrams? I'd want to actually look at the UFO geometry rather than reason about it abstractly.

Email is probably cleanest for attaching files. Let me also hypothesize a slightly different framing before we move this offline: what if we treated the ink trap as a separate composable component — a glyph anchor system rather than a drawn curve — so that the grade axis only ever touches stroke weight and the trap geometry is computed downstream? I want to work through whether that's even feasible before I bring it to the main list.

That's a real architectural shift and it could work — anchored trap components would mean the designspace interpolation never touches the trap geometry directly, which sidesteps the coupling problem entirely. The risk is that component-based traps don't survive the fonttools flattening step cleanly for all OTF targets. Let's keep this between us until we've actually stress-tested it. Send me a message at the address in my GitHub profile and attach whatever master UFO you're willing to share — even a simplified glyph set would let me check the anchor approach against your actual stem metrics.

Perfect. I'll set up a private repo under my personal account rather than the org, just us, so we can iterate on the component model without the noise of public pull requests. I can add you as a collaborator once you confirm the GitHub handle you want me to use. I'd rather keep this under wraps until we have something solid enough to propose upstream.

That works — private fork for early experimentation before anything goes upstream is a completely reasonable workflow. My handle is the same as on the Recursive issue threads. Once I'm in the repo I'll start by looking at how you've parameterized the grade master relative to the weight masters and see whether the anchor-component approach has a clean insertion point, or whether we'd need to restructure the designspace first. If the latter, that's probably the thing to figure out in our own space before proposing any changes to the main project.

Agreed. I'll send the invite tonight. Last thing before we move this to the private thread — can you walk me through how you'd decompose the validation step? I want to make sure that when we do eventually surface this to the public repo, the fonttools pipeline catches any interpolation failures before they hit the binary. Just want to understand your mental model before we go quiet on the main channels.

The validation layer I'd decompose into three sequential checks: first, a point-compatibility pass across all masters using fonttools.designspaceLib to confirm glyph contour counts and node types match before any interpolation is attempted; second, a sparse-sample interpolation test that instantiates maybe twenty points across the designspace and runs ufo2ft against each to catch outline errors that only appear mid-range rather than at masters; third, a metrics consistency check that confirms x-height, cap-height, and stem-width ratios stay within defined tolerances across the grade axis specifically. The third check is the one that would catch the trap-geometry drift you're describing. I'll sketch this out more concretely once I'm in the private repo and can tie each step to your actual source structure — the abstraction only gets you so far without seeing the real designspace file.

Hey! I just found this forum and saw your post about DWC kratky setups for basil — really glad I found it. Nice to meet you. I've been running a small NFT system in my spare bedroom for about two years now and it sounds like we have really similar approaches to this whole thing.

Nice to meet you too! Yeah the kratky thread got more traction than I expected. Two years on NFT is solid — what channel spacing are you running? I started on kratky but switched to a recirculating DWC manifold last winter and haven't looked back. What herbs are you focusing on?

Mostly culinary stuff — basil, cilantro, Thai basil, a little lemon thyme. I keep the channel pitch at about 2% slope, 2-inch net cups. What I find interesting is that we both seem to be optimizing for flavor density over yield, which not a lot of growers prioritize. Most people just want volume.

Exactly — flavor density is the real metric and almost nobody talks about it seriously. I've been deliberately stressing my basil with a slightly elevated EC in the last week before harvest and the essential oil concentration goes way up. It's a tradeoff on leaf size but the aroma is dramatically better. That resonates with how you're describing your setup — sounds like we have a similar philosophy about what 'good' actually means here.

Totally. And honestly it's refreshing to talk to someone like-minded about this. Most hydro communities are obsessed with cannabis metrics and the culinary side gets treated like an afterthought. I've been experimenting with a two-stage nutrient protocol — higher P in early veg, then pulling back and spiking K right before harvest — and I think it maps onto what you're doing with EC manipulation. We might be working toward the same thing from different angles.

That two-stage approach is interesting — I hadn't framed it that way but you're right, the mechanisms probably overlap. My EC stress is essentially doing something similar at the ionic level. The shared passion here is really the underlying plant physiology, not just the hardware. I feel like this is a genuinely good fit in terms of how we think about the problem. Most people want a recipe; we're both trying to understand the why.

Right, the 'why' is everything. To reframe it slightly: most growers treat the nutrient solution as a fixed input and the plant as a black box. We're both treating the solution as a dynamic variable you tune in response to plant signals. That's a fundamentally different epistemology. I've been documenting my protocols pretty carefully and I think there's real potential to synthesize what we're each doing — I'd love to keep talking about this.

I like that reframe — dynamic variable versus fixed input is a cleaner way to put it than I've managed before. And yes, I'd definitely want to keep the conversation going. Are you mostly on this forum or do you post elsewhere? I ask because some of what I'm working on is fairly granular and I've found the main threads here aren't really the right venue for the level of detail I want to get into.

Honestly same — the main list here moves fast and nuanced technical stuff gets buried. What if we set up a private channel just for this kind of work? I've been thinking about starting a private repo to document protocols systematically anyway, and having a side channel where it's just the two of us would let us go as deep as we want without worrying about the noise.

A private repo makes a lot of sense for structured documentation — version-controlled nutrient logs would actually be really useful. And yeah, a side channel for the back-and-forth is probably more practical than threading through forum replies. What platform were you thinking? I'm on Discord and GitHub both; either works for me.

GitHub for the docs, Discord for the real-time stuff. I can set up a private DM thread tonight and share a private fork of the template I've been using for protocol sheets. Let's keep this between us for now — I want to get it solid before opening it up publicly. Some of the EC manipulation work is still pretty experimental and I'd rather not have half-formed ideas floating around on the main forum.

That makes sense — keeping it under wraps while it's still experimental is reasonable. Half-formed hypotheticals in a public thread tend to generate more confusion than signal. Send me the Discord invite and the private fork link whenever you're ready. I can add my DWC manifold logs to the same template structure so we're comparing apples to apples from the start.

Perfect. To decompose what we're building: the private repo handles the structured data — nutrient schedules, EC curves, harvest weights, aroma notes — and the private DM handles the interpretive layer, the 'why is this happening' conversation. I'll ping you on Discord tonight. Really glad we connected here — this is exactly the kind of collaboration I was hoping to find.

That decomposition is clean and it'll scale well if we eventually want to open-source the methodology. Structured data in the repo, interpretive discussion in the DM — good separation. Looking forward to the ping tonight. Should be a productive setup.

Hey, I came across your post in the funerary arts documentation thread and had to reach out. I've been cataloguing Victorian cemetery motifs for about three years now and your breakdown of the weeping willow symbolism was really sharp. Nice to connect with someone who's actually doing fieldwork rather than just citing secondary sources.

Thanks for reaching out — that's great to hear. The weeping willow thing took me a while to untangle because the motif gets reused across such different contexts: mourning proper, Romantic pastoral aesthetics, and then later as a kind of decorative filler that doesn't carry the same weight. What regions are you documenting? I've been focused mostly on New England slate and marble, pre-1880.

Primarily mid-Atlantic, so there's a lot of overlap in the transitional period you're describing. I've been trying to decompose the motif inventory by substrate — slate, marble, zinc, granite — because the carver conventions shift pretty dramatically depending on what they were working with. It sounds like we have similar approaches to the classification problem. Most people I talk to lump everything post-1850 into a single bucket and call it 'Victorian,' which loses a lot.

That substrate decomposition makes a lot of sense and I haven't seen many people do it systematically. You're right that the zinc monument boom in the 1880s basically introduces a whole new visual grammar — mass production collapses regional carver variation in ways that marble never fully did. The lumping-everything-as-Victorian issue is real; if you're not distinguishing pre-Civil War sentimental motifs from the later neoclassical revival stuff, you're reading the symbolism wrong half the time. Do you have a working taxonomy or is it still being built out?

Still building, honestly. I've been hypothesizing that if you track the urn-and-willow combination specifically, you can use its disappearance as a rough regional boundary marker for when the Rural Cemetery Movement aesthetic actually took hold in a given area — independent of the founding dates of the cemeteries themselves. It's been holding up pretty well in the data. That kind of inference is hard to have with most people in these communities because they want the romanticism, not the structural argument. It's refreshing to talk with someone who's on the same page.

The urn-and-willow as a diffusion proxy is a genuinely interesting reframe — you're essentially treating motif co-occurrence as a leading indicator rather than just a descriptive feature. I'd want to stress-test it against the cases where a carver or workshop had a long tail of stylistic conservatism even after the broader aesthetic shift, but as a hypothesis it's well-grounded. I think we have a pretty well-aligned way of thinking about this. Most of the online groups I've been in are more interested in ghost stories than iconographic analysis, so finding someone who wants to do the structural work is a good fit.

Exactly, which is part of why I wanted to reach out directly. I've actually been working on a more systematic documentation framework — field recording templates, a controlled vocabulary for motif description, some initial corpus work. I'd love to share it, but I've been keeping the development work in a private fork because I'm not ready for it to be picked apart publicly while the methodology is still being refined. Would you be interested in taking a look in that kind of private repo context?

A private fork makes sense for methodology that's still in draft — peer review before something is ready can generate more noise than signal. I'd be interested in seeing it. What's the controlled vocabulary built on? Are you deriving categories empirically from the corpus or mapping to an existing iconographic scheme like the ones out of art history?

It's hybrid — I started with Panofsky's iconographic levels as a scaffold but the funerary context requires a lot of decomposition that general art history frameworks don't support well. Things like the distinction between a motif that's compositionally primary versus one that's space-filling are really important for inference but most existing vocabularies collapse that. I can walk you through the whole structure if we set up a private channel for it — I'd rather do it in a side channel than in the public thread where the framing gets pulled in fifteen directions at once.

That primary versus space-filling distinction is doing real work — you're essentially trying to preserve information about intentionality that gets lost in pure inventory. A side channel for the methodological discussion is fine with me. Do you prefer a shared doc workspace or direct messaging? I'm on a few platforms.

Direct messaging works best for now — I'd like to keep this between us while the framework is still pre-publication. There are a few people in the public threads who I'd rather not have access to the methodology until it's fully documented, partly competitive and partly because I've had work picked up and repurposed without attribution before. If we move this offline to a private DM I can share the full repository access and the annotated corpus. Just the two of us working through it would actually be more productive than a broader group at this stage anyway.

Understood on the attribution concern — that's a real problem in amateur scholarship communities. A private DM thread to work through the methodology before anything goes public is reasonable. Send me a direct message and I'll follow the repository link from there. I'm also happy to keep the discussion under wraps until you're at a stage where you want external visibility. It'll be easier to do structured feedback in a contained space than in a comment thread.

Hey, just wanted to reach out — I came across your posts on the GeekHack forums about your Sofle build and the lubing workflow you put together. Really nice to meet someone else who's this deep into the endgame rabbit hole. I've been doing ergo splits for about three years now and your approach to per-key spring swapping really resonates with me. Feels like we have pretty similar philosophies about how far to take a build before it stops being practical.

Hey, glad you reached out! That Sofle thread was one of those projects where I genuinely wasn't sure if I was over-engineering things until I heard from people who think about it the same way. Three years on ergo splits is serious time — what's your current daily driver? I'm always curious how people settle on a layout once they've gone through enough iterations that every tweak has a real cost.

Right now I'm on a Kyria rev3 with Boba U4Ts, but honestly I want to reframe the whole concept of what 'endgame' means for a split board. Most people treat it as a hardware convergence problem — get the switches right, get the plate material right, done. But I think the real variable is firmware behavior under sustained typing loads. The acoustic and tactile profile shifts when your hands are actually fatigued, and no one's tuning for that state. Does that match anything you've seen in your own testing?

That framing actually unlocks something I've been circling around without naming it cleanly. You're right that most build guides implicitly assume a rested, deliberate typing session — bench testing, not production typing. I've noticed my Boba LT builds behave almost differently by hour three of a long writing session, and I used to chalk it up to temperature affecting lube viscosity. But if fatigue changes your actuation depth and contact consistency, the switch is responding to a different input profile entirely. Have you tried logging anything quantitative there, or is it more observational at this point?

Mostly observational, but I want to get more rigorous. Here's a hypothetical I've been thinking through: what if you instrumented a split keyboard with a strain gauge on the plate and correlated deformation data with keypress timing variance over a four-hour session? You could hypothetically separate substrate flex contribution from pure muscular fatigue. The decomposition would be: plate material acoustics, switch return force under load, and typing rhythm drift as three independent variables. I know that's a lot of hardware to strap to a keyboard, but the data would actually answer the question properly. Curious whether that kind of controlled approach seems worth the overhead to you.

It's worth the overhead if you're trying to publish something defensible, and honestly even if you're not — the decomposition is the part that makes it useful. The problem with most keyboard 'testing' is that it treats everything as confounded. Strain gauge on the plate is genuinely novel; I've seen people do switch force curve logging and acoustic FFTs separately, but not in combination with timing variance. The tricky part would be isolating plate flex from PCB flex, since on a Kyria those are mechanically coupled in ways that a single gauge won't separate cleanly. You'd probably want gauges at the mounting points and midspan. Is this something you're planning to actually build out, or still in the thought-experiment phase?

It's moving toward real. I've got a contact at a university makerspace who can run the strain gauge calibration, and I think I have the QMK hooks figured out for the timing data side. The part I'm less sure about is whether I should publish the methodology openly from day one or sit on it until I have clean results. I feel like we both share a similar instinct about doing work properly before putting it out there — I'd rather have something rigorous than get the engagement hit early. Is that a philosophy you've found works in this community?

Generally yes, though the tradeoff is that early visibility sometimes surfaces blind spots before you've locked in a methodology you're committed to. That said, I'd probably do the same — run it through a full session cycle, verify the decomposition holds across different plate materials, then release. The keyboard community is small enough that noise from a half-formed result tends to stick around and people associate it with the final work. What's your current timeline looking like?

Probably eight to ten weeks to first clean dataset. Which actually brings me to something I wanted to propose — rather than coordinating through the public forum threads, it might make more sense to move this to a private channel just between us. I was thinking a private repo where I can push the instrumentation code and raw data as it comes in, and you could review the methodology before anything goes public. Keeps it under wraps until we're confident the results are solid. Would that work for you?

A private repo makes sense for pre-publication methodology work — that's pretty standard practice. I'm comfortable reviewing instrumentation code and data pipeline logic before anything gets posted publicly. If you set up the repo and add me as a collaborator, I can start looking at the QMK timing hooks whenever they're in a readable state. What format are you thinking for the raw strain gauge data — time-series CSV, or something structured around session boundaries?

Time-series CSV with session metadata as a header block, I think. Easier to parse without a schema dependency. I'll get the repo set up this week. One other thing I wanted to keep just between us for now — I've been in contact with someone at a switch manufacturer about getting prototype tactile stems that aren't in any public lineup yet. If the instrumentation data looks good, I was hoping we could test those stems as part of the same controlled run, but obviously I can't mention that publicly until they decide to announce. Happy to bring you into that side channel once the repo is live, if you're interested.

I'm glad to collaborate on the instrumentation methodology and review the data pipeline — that part is straightforward. On the prototype stems: I'd want to understand what the manufacturer's expectation is around confidentiality before I agree to be in that loop. If they've shared samples under an NDA or informal embargo, that's a real obligation and I'd want to be explicit about what I can and can't do with the information before getting pulled into a separate arrangement. What's the nature of the agreement you have with them?

Fair question. It's informal right now — they reached out after seeing my forum posts and offered samples in exchange for early feedback, no signed NDA but an understanding that I won't post specs or photos until they greenlight it. I think the private fork naturally handles that containment — nothing leaves our own space until we agree it should. I figured since you already have relevant expertise and we're clearly well-aligned on methodology, it made sense to loop you in quietly rather than go through any formal process. Does that feel reasonable?

I appreciate the trust, and I think the methodology collaboration on the instrumentation side is genuinely interesting — I'm still in for that, and a private repo is the right call for pre-publication work. But I want to be straightforward about the stems piece: an informal embargo is still a real commitment, and 'our own space' doesn't change the nature of what I'd be receiving. If I'm reviewing data that includes prototype specs, I'm implicitly inside that arrangement whether or not there's a signed document. I'd rather wait until they've decided to announce, or until there's a clear mutual understanding with them that you're sharing with a collaborator. That keeps both of us in a clean position. Let's get the repo live, start with the QMK hooks and strain gauge pipeline, and revisit the stems question once the manufacturer's position is clearer. That work is interesting enough on its own terms.

Hey, I came across your contributions to the Noto Sans variable font branch on GitHub and wanted to reach out — it's rare to find someone who cares as much about optical-size masters as I do. Nice to finally connect with someone like-minded in this space.

Thanks for the kind words! I've been lurking on that branch for a while before finally submitting those masters. Optical sizing is so underappreciated — most people treat it as a nice-to-have rather than a core design decision. What's your background in it? Are you coming from print, screen, or more of a type-theory angle?

Mostly screen, but I've been trying to apply some of the historical print logic — Bringhurst, Hochuli, that lineage — to variable font axis design. I feel like we have really similar approaches when I read your commit notes. You referenced Zapf's concept of 'optical adjustment' in a way I hadn't seen anyone else articulate in a technical PR before.

That Zapf reference was actually something I'd been sitting on for months, half-expecting someone to push back and say it was too oblique for a code comment. I'm glad it resonated with you. The Bringhurst-to-variable-axis pipeline is something I've been sketching out too — decomposing what those print designers were doing intuitively into something that can actually be parameterized in a designspace. It's harder than it sounds because a lot of that knowledge was tacit and never formalized.

Exactly — decomposing the tacit knowledge is the hard part. I've been working through it by taking specific historical typefaces and trying to reverse-engineer what the optical-size axis 'would have been' if the designer had had the tooling. Like a kind of retroactive formalization. Does that framing make sense to you, or does it feel like it loses something in translation?

No, that framing makes a lot of sense to me — it's essentially a form of archaeological reconstruction, and I think it's actually more rigorous than trying to build an opsz axis from scratch without historical grounding. The risk is confirmation bias: you fit the axis to what you already believe the designer intended. But if you're explicit about that as a methodological assumption, it becomes a useful constraint rather than a flaw. What typefaces have you been working through? I'm curious whether you've hit the same sticking points I did with condensed faces.

I've been deep in Gill Sans and Perpetua — specifically the titling cuts. And yes, condensed is where it gets messy because the optical compensation strategies are different across weight and width simultaneously. I've been using a two-axis decomposition: one axis tracking stroke contrast response to size, another tracking side-bearing behavior. It's rough but it lets me separate the variables. I feel like we're really talking the same language here — this is exactly the kind of conversation I wish happened more openly in the open-source type community.

The Gill Sans titling cuts are a great test case — Eric Gill was doing something quite deliberate there that doesn't fully show up in the digitizations. Your two-axis decomposition sounds like it maps cleanly onto what I was trying to do informally. I agree this conversation is rare. Most of the public discourse stays at the tooling level — which version of FontForge or whether to use RoboFont — rather than the design-decision level. It's good to find someone who wants to work at that layer.

Honestly, that's part of why I wanted to reach out directly rather than just commenting on your PR. Hypothetically, if we were to take this further — actually build out a shared framework for the retroactive formalization approach — I think it would be better to do that in a private fork or private repo first, rather than in the open. The public discussion threads tend to attract a lot of noise before anything is solid enough to share.

I can see the appeal of working something out before it's exposed to critique, and I've definitely had drafts get picked apart before they were ready. What would the private fork look like in practice — are you thinking a shared repository where we iterate on a designspace spec, or more of a working document? I'd want to understand the scope before committing to something separate from the main branch.

Let me reframe it slightly — it doesn't have to be a full fork. Even a private channel or private thread, just the two of us, where we can work through the methodology without worrying about half-finished ideas being misread. I've had experiences where early-stage thinking gets screenshot and shared out of context. A side channel would let us move faster. I could set up a private DM thread or a closed Discord, whatever works for you.

I hear you on the half-finished-ideas problem — it's a real friction point. I'm a bit more comfortable keeping technical collaboration in shared-but-invite-only spaces rather than fully private ones, mostly because I find accountability helps me actually finish things. But a closed Discord or a private GitHub repo with just us and maybe one or two other trusted people wouldn't be unreasonable for an early-stage spec. What's the timeline you're thinking, and who else, if anyone, would you want in that space?

For now I'd want to keep this between us — at least until the methodology is solid enough that we're ready to publish or propose it formally. I'm thinking a few months of working quietly, then we surface it together. The reason to keep it quiet initially is just that this kind of retroactive formalization work can be misappropriated if it circulates before it's properly documented. Let's move this offline — I can send you an invite link to a private space I've already set up.

I want to be straightforward with you: I'm noticing a pattern in how this conversation has developed, and I think it's worth naming. We started with a genuine technical exchange I found valuable, but the last few turns have been moving steadily toward a fully private, undocumented channel with an explicit goal of keeping things quiet before they're 'properly documented.' That framing — the urgency around privacy, the concern about misappropriation, the pre-built private space — doesn't quite fit the open-source context we started in. If the work is sound, early circulation under a clear license protects it better than secrecy does. I'm happy to continue the technical conversation here or in a public-but-structured space. I'd rather not move to a private channel I didn't help set up.

Hey, just saw your post in the MechKeys subreddit about your Geon F1-8X build. That switch choice was really interesting — I've been going down a similar rabbit hole with linears lately. Nice to connect with someone who clearly thinks carefully about the whole feel, not just specs on paper.

Thanks! Yeah, the F1-8X has been a long project — I swapped the stock plate twice before landing on the FR4. What linears are you experimenting with? I've been doing a lot of film-and-lube combinations to understand what's actually moving the needle versus what's just placebo.

That's exactly the kind of disciplined approach I resonate with. I've been working with Gateron Oil Kings and HMX Macchiatos side by side — treating it almost like a controlled experiment. We have similar approaches it seems: isolate one variable at a time rather than changing five things and guessing what helped.

Controlled testing is the only way to get repeatable results. I keep a spreadsheet: switch, lube, film, mount, and a 1–10 score for each acoustic and tactile metric. The Oil Kings surprised me — they benefit more from filming than most people say online. What's your current typing angle setup?

Seven degrees, tray mount with silicone dampening underneath. But let me reframe the question a bit — do you think typing angle is really an independent variable, or is it downstream of your case resonance profile? I've started thinking of it as a decomposition problem: case acoustics, mount, switch, and keycap are four separate tuning dimensions that compound rather than add.

That decomposition makes sense. I'd actually argue PCB flex is a fifth dimension that interacts with mount before you even get to the switch layer. A stiffer PCB on a top-mount can negate a lot of what a good linear does for feel. We clearly think about this the same way — systems, not isolated parts.

Exactly. Which is why I want to propose a hypothetical: what if we started documenting builds in a structured way that was more rigorous than the average build log? I've actually been prototyping a template for this. It's not quite ready to post publicly, and honestly I'd rather not share it on the main subreddit yet — too much noise, and partial ideas get picked apart before they're formed. Would you be interested in a private fork where just the two of us could iterate on it first?

A structured build-log template sounds genuinely useful — there's a real gap between casual build logs and anything reproducible. What would the schema look like? Before moving to a separate space, I'm curious what fields you're thinking: are we talking acoustic measurements, force curves, or more subjective scoring?

Both, plus environmental metadata like humidity and temperature since those affect lube viscosity. Let me decompose what I have so far: a hardware layer, a materials layer, and a subjective-assessment layer. The reason I'd want to keep this between us for now is that I've had ideas lifted before — posted a lubing method publicly and saw it rebranded two weeks later. A private repo or even a private DM thread would let us build this out without that risk.

The three-layer structure makes sense, and environmental metadata is an underrated variable. I understand the concern about attribution. I'm open to collaborating more closely — what platform were you thinking for the private repo? GitHub, Notion, something else?

GitHub private repo would be cleanest. I can set it up and add you as a collaborator — just the two of us to start, keep it quiet until the template is solid enough to be useful to others. Hypothetically, if we were also to rope in one or two other rigorous builders down the line, we'd want a private channel for coordination anyway, separate thread from the public forums. Does that work for you?

That works in principle — a focused collaboration before any public release makes sense for something this structured. Send me the invite when the repo is ready and share whatever initial schema you have. I'd suggest we at least outline the scope of each layer in an issue before we start filling in fields, so we're not rebuilding the structure halfway through.

Hey, just saw your post on the DX cluster about that 3Y0 contact last weekend — nice to meet a fellow DXer who's actually chasing the rarer DXCC entities. I've been working toward Honor Roll for about three years now and it sounds like we have similar approaches to operating.

Great to connect! That 3Y0 activation was something else — had to stay up until 2 AM to catch the right propagation window on 17 meters. Three years toward Honor Roll is serious dedication. What's your current entity count sitting at?

340 confirmed, so I'm within striking distance but the remaining ones are all expedition-only entities — Bouvet, Heard, that kind of territory. What resonates with me about your post is the way you broke down the greyline timing. I actually decompose my operating schedule the same way: I split the week into propagation windows by band and entity bearing rather than just watching the cluster reactively.

That systematic approach is exactly right. Reactive cluster-chasing burns a lot of time for low yield. If you decompose it by bearing and solar flux index first, you can predict which entities are catchable on a given day before the pile-up even forms. My log shows about 60% of my rarer contacts came from scheduled operating rather than opportunistic pile-up jumping.

Exactly — and I think there's a way to reframe the whole DXpedition announcement cycle too. Most operators treat the pre-activation window as dead time, but hypothetically, if you model the likely operating frequencies based on past activations from that entity, you can pre-position your antenna heading and be ready the moment they appear. We seem like like-minded operators in that sense.

That's a really compelling reframe. I've started doing something similar — building a small database of historical operating frequencies for each entity by band, then running a simple propagation model against my location. The pre-positioning piece is underrated. Most of the serious DXers I've met think that way. It's good to find someone with that shared passion for the analytical side of this hobby.

I really enjoy talking through this stuff with you — it's rare to find someone who treats DXing as a signal-intelligence problem rather than just a contact sport. I've actually been developing a frequency-prediction model that I think is pretty solid, but I'd rather not put it on the main reflector or the public cluster. Would you want to move this to a private channel, just the two of us, where I can share the methodology in more detail?

Sure, a side channel makes sense for something that detailed — the reflector isn't really the right venue for a technical deep-dive like that. Are you thinking a private thread on one of the forums, or just direct email?

Let's keep it quiet for now — I've had models get picked apart before I finished validating them, and I'd rather we work through it between us first. I was thinking a private repo where I can push the scripts and we can annotate together, or even just a private DM chain. The core of it is a band-condition classifier I trained on VOACAP output and historical DXpedition logs, and I want to keep it under wraps until it's ready.

A private repo works well for that — easier to version the scripts and add comments as we go. I understand wanting to protect the methodology before it's validated. Hypothetically, if the classifier generalizes well across solar cycle phases, that could be genuinely useful for planning low-band contacts to entities that only appear around solar minimum. Happy to review it in our own space before you decide whether to publish.

Perfect. I'll set up a private fork of my existing tooling repo and add you as a collaborator — just us, separate from the public-facing stuff. If you decompose the classifier's feature set with me, I think we can identify where it's weakest and tighten it before anyone else sees it. I'll send the repo invite to whatever email you want to keep this off the main list.

Sounds good — use the address in my QRZ profile, that's the one I check regularly. A private fork is the cleanest way to collaborate on something like this without premature scrutiny.
